concept.logline: A crime-scene cleaner who scrubs death out of strangers' homes becomes fixated on the grieving widower whose wife's blood she was hired to erase—until she realizes the spatter she cleaned doesn't match the suicide he reported, and that the only person who can prove he's a murderer is the woman who destroyed the evidence.
concept.premise: Nadia Vos runs a one-woman biohazard remediation business in Rotterdam—the person families call after the police tape comes down, the one who kneels in other people's worst days and makes the horror disappear for a flat fee and a signed non-disclosure. She is good at it because she feels nothing at the scenes; the numbness is the point, the reason she chose the work. Then she's hired to clean the study of Sébastien Aro, a beloved cookbook author whose wife Margaux "shot herself" while he was at a book signing across town. Nadia spends eleven hours alone in the dead woman's house, and by hour three she is no longer cleaning—she is reading it. The angle of the spatter. The single wiped floorboard nobody asked her to wipe. The way the widower's voice on the phone is too composed, then breaks in exactly the wrong place. She should report it. Instead she keeps the house key she was supposed to return, lets herself back in, and starts arranging encounters—grief-support coffees, a delivered casserole, a slow embedding of herself into Sébastien's ruined life. She tells herself it's to catch a killer. But the reader watches her linger in Margaux's closet, wear Margaux's coat, answer Margaux's still-active phone. Nadia isn't investigating a widower. She's auditioning for a dead woman's life—and the more she wants Sébastien, the less she can trust a single thing her own narration tells her about what she really saw on that floor.
concept.obsession_engine: The obsession is proximity weaponized twice over. Nadia's occupation grants her the most intimate access imaginable—she is alone in the house with the physical residue of a person's death, handling the mattress, the clothes, the private mess no spouse or detective ever sees, under a legal gag order that guarantees no one will ever ask what she noticed. That access metastasizes into fixation: she doesn't fall for a man, she falls for the ERASED WOMAN first (Margaux's handwriting, her half-finished manuscript, the life Nadia is literally wiping away) and then transfers that longing onto the widower as the last living door back into it. The toxic-intimacy voltage is the "I shouldn't want this and I want it more because I shouldn't"—she is sexually and emotionally drawn to the exact man she believes is a murderer, and the danger is the aphrodisiac, not the deterrent. The engine drives every chapter because Nadia's job is professionally required forgetting, and obsession is compulsive remembering; she is at war with her own trained numbness, and each time she chooses to remember one more detail she loses one more layer of the protective deadness that kept her sane. The book is the sound of a woman deliberately taking herself apart to feel something, using someone else's death as the crowbar.
concept.protagonist_engine: Nadia Vos, 39, is the anti-caretaker: where the housemaid trope gives us a servant who wants to belong, Nadia is the person who arrives precisely because belonging has ended—she is intimacy's undertaker. We root for her despite ourselves because her voice is bone-dry, unsentimental, blackly funny about gore, and armored in competence; she is the only calm person in every room she enters, and that control is magnetic right up until we clock that it's dissociation, not strength. She reveals early, flatly, that she took this job after cleaning a scene that was personal to her (a detail she narrates around, never through—the psychological opacity), and the reader slowly understands her numbness is a wound, not a personality. She's repeatable as a series lead because her PROFESSION is an engine: she will always be handed access to strangers' deaths, always be gagged from speaking, always be the one person positioned to notice the thing that doesn't add up—and always the one whose damage makes her the least reliable witness to it. She is competent enough to investigate, broken enough to be wrong about herself, and self-aware enough to know both. That triangle is the whole appeal.
concept.antagonist_engine: Sébastien Aro is the primary opposing force and the object of obsession fused into one body—the more Nadia pursues intimacy with him, the more each gained inch of closeness could be either exoneration or a killer reeling in the one witness who can bury him. He is charming, wounded, plausibly innocent, and he begins to obsess back, which is where the power-inversion bites: the access-holder (Nadia) thinks she has the upper hand because she has the secret, but Sébastien, if guilty, holds the deadlier card—he knows a crime-scene cleaner who destroyed evidence and then stalked her way into his bed is nobody a jury will believe. The deeper antagonist, though, is the truth itself and Nadia's own unreliable perception: the book's real adversary is the question of whether the spatter evidence exists as she remembers it, or whether a woman desperate to matter has authored a murder to give herself a reason to stay. Margaux—dead, brilliant, everywhere in the house—functions as a third antagonistic presence, a ghost-rival Nadia can never win against and can't stop trying to become.
concept.central_threat: A possible undetected murder Nadia cannot report without confessing she destroyed the evidence and illegally re-entered the home—so proof and self-incrimination are the same act. The escalating danger: if Sébastien killed Margaux, Nadia has inserted herself into a murderer's reach as the sole inconvenient witness; if he didn't, Nadia is the dangerous one, a woman falsifying a crime to justify an obsession, spiraling toward doing to Sébastien's next relationship what she imagines was done to Margaux. Either way, someone in this house dies before it ends, and the mystery engine is: whose memory of that floor is telling the truth.
concept.stakes: Personal and mortal. If Nadia is right and stays silent, she is complicit in a killing and next in line. If she is wrong, she is destroying an innocent grieving man and confirming she is the monster she's spent a career hiding from—her sanity, her freedom (evidence tampering is prison), and her last thread of self-belief are all on the table. Emotionally, the stakes are her numbness: to prove anything she must let herself fully feel, and feeling is the thing that once nearly killed her. And a living stake walks the page—Camille, Margaux's teenage sister, who trusts Nadia and will inherit whatever version of the truth Nadia decides to make real.
concept.escalation_pattern: ACT 1 (access): The clean; the wrong spatter; keeping the key. Escalates from professional detachment to the first illicit re-entry—Nadia crosses from witness to intruder. Chapter hooks on physical evidence details that don't fit. ACT 2A (embedding): Engineered proximity to Sébastien—grief group, casseroles, then real desire. Escalation via intimacy: every closer moment is both more tender and more dangerous, and Nadia starts editing her own narration (gaps, contradictions the reader begins to catch). ACT 2B (inversion): Sébastien turns the access around—he knows what she is, invites her deeper, and we can't tell if it's love, entrapment, or a killer's containment strategy. Nadia finds a second scene detail that flips the case; then finds evidence it flips right back. Escalation via epistemics: the ground under every "fact" liquefies. ACT 3 (collapse): The two readings of the floor become mutually exclusive and both are supported by planted clues; a confrontation forces the memory to resolve; someone dies; Nadia must choose which truth to make permanent—and the choice indicts her. Tension ratchets by shrinking the gap between "she's the hunter" and "she's the danger" until they occupy the same sentence.
concept.twist_architecture: Twin twist, both earned on the page. TWIST ONE (the case): Margaux's death was neither the reported suicide nor Sébastien's murder—Margaux staged her own suicide to look like a murder in order to frame the husband who'd spent a decade erasing her (taking credit for her recipes, her writing, her whole authored self). She chose to hire, specifically, a crime-scene cleaner via a pre-arranged instruction, betting that the one person legally silenced and professionally trained to notice anomalies would preserve the "wrongness" as suspicion. Nadia wasn't drawn to the scene by instinct; she was CAST in it by a dead woman who understood exactly how obsession and access work. Planted clues: the single unasked-for wiped floorboard (Margaux's deliberate signature, not a killer's error); the still-active phone with scheduled messages; the manuscript pointedly left where a cleaner, not a cop, would find it; Margaux's research—seeded early as a throwaway—into forensic remediation. TWIST TWO (the narrator): Nadia's foundational trauma, the "personal scene" she narrates around all book, was her OWN sister's death, which she cleaned herself and has spent years telling herself was suicide—and the reader realizes Nadia latched onto Margaux's staged frame because she is desperate for one death, any death, to finally not be a suicide she failed to prevent. The psychological opacity pays off: she wasn't lying to us, she was withholding from herself. The two twists interlock—Margaux weaponized a stranger's exact wound to make herself unforgettable, and Nadia let her, because being haunted was preferable to being numb. Shocking, but on reread every beat is load-bearing.
concept.series_engine: Standalone-complete: this book's murder, twist, and Nadia's reckoning fully resolve. The repeatable engine is the OCCUPATION, not a couple or a cliffhanger. Nadia Vos, crime-scene cleaner, is a franchise character in the mold of a Cormoran Strike or a Dexter-adjacent brand: every book hands her a new home, a new death she was paid to erase, a new NDA that makes her the only silenced witness, and a new stranger she gets too close to. Connected-standalone structure—Book 2 (a hoarder's death that wasn't natural), Book 3 (a "drowning" in a canal house)—each a self-contained obsession-thriller sharing Nadia, her deepening relationship with her own damage, and a slow-burn series arc about whether feeling again saves or destroys her. Author-brand scalable: "The Cleaner" as a series title line, instantly legible cover system, rapid-release friendly because the engine resets cleanly each book without requiring readers to have read the last.
concept.why_buy_book_one: The hook is a killer elevator pitch a reader repeats verbatim: the woman who erases crime scenes falls for the widower whose wife's blood she cleaned—and the evidence she destroyed is the only proof he's a murderer. It delivers the exact dark-romance voltage BookTok chases (forbidden desire, danger-as-aphrodisiac, morally-grey narrator) with none of the genre's safety net—no promised HEA, a real twist as the payoff. The occupation is fresh and viscerally cinematic (nobody's done the crime-scene cleaner as obsessive narrator), the setting is contemporary and cheap to research, and the voice—dry, competent, quietly coming apart—is the kind readers quote. It scratches You, Verity, and The Silent Patient in one buy.
concept.why_continue: Book 1 closes the case but cracks Nadia open—she ends changed, no longer safely numb, which makes the next death she's hired to clean a genuine threat to a woman who now feels everything. Readers who fell for her voice will want the next house, the next stranger, the next test of whether she can be near death without dissolving into it. The series promise is clean and addictive: same unforgettable narrator, same occupation-engine, brand-new self-contained obsession every book—McFadden-style bingeability on a franchise character, not a soap-opera cliffhanger.
concept.booktok_hook: She's the woman they hire to clean up after a murder. She fell for the man whose wife's blood she scrubbed off the floor—and the evidence she destroyed is the only proof he did it.
concept.buildability_notes: Solo-indie friendly. Contemporary single-city setting (Rotterdam, or swap to any mid-size city—Portland, Manchester, Antwerp—with zero plot impact); no period, procedural, or institutional gatekeeper research required. Research burden is light and front-loadable: a few hours reading first-person accounts and trade blogs on biohazard/crime-scene remediation for authentic texture (the enzymes, the ozone machines, the NDAs, the psychology of the trade), plus surface-level blood-spatter basics—enough to sell it, and the unreliable narrator conveniently insulates any forensic imprecision (her readings are subjective by design). Small cast (Nadia, Sébastien, Margaux-in-absentia, Camille, one detective, one confidant), largely single-location, dialogue-and-interiority driven—cheap to write fast. Target 82-88k, 55-65 short chapters each ending on a hook (a found detail, a narration gap, a lie caught). Cover system is instantly brandable: latex glove, a single drop, biohazard-yellow accent, the tagline line. Rapid-release ready.
concept.originality_claim: The obsession-thriller access role has been done as nanny, housekeeper, nurse, assistant—all people who enter a life to serve it. Nadia enters AFTER the life ends, to erase it: the caretaker of aftermath, an occupation with built-in, legally-enforced silence that makes her the structurally perfect unreliable witness—no one is allowed to ask what she saw, so we only have her word. That's genuinely fresh versus the comps. The power-inversion is doubled and new: the access-holder believes possessing the secret gives her power, when in fact destroying the evidence has made her the one person who can never safely tell the truth. And the twist re-architects the trope's usual "servant infiltrates family" into "a dead woman deliberately cast a stranger as her avenger"—the object of obsession authored the obsession from beyond the grave, exploiting the exact psychology the sub-genre runs on. It weds You's predatory-narrator intimacy to The Silent Patient's engineered-frame reveal, through an occupation neither has touched, with no romance-genre HEA guardrail.

concept.logline: A grief-stricken data annotator who has memorized every frame of a wellness influencer's life talks her way into becoming the woman's live-in assistant—only to realize the influencer has been curating her feed to lure exactly one kind of viewer, and she qualified.
concept.premise: Wren Delacroix, 29, spends her nights labeling training data for a content-moderation AI—flagging violence, self-harm, and abuse frame by frame, ten hours a shift, until other people's worst moments feel more real than her own life. Her one comfort is @honestlyeloise, a soft-lit wellness influencer with 1.2 million followers whose vulnerability posts ("the truth about my miscarriage," "why I left him") feel addressed to Wren personally. Wren doesn't just watch Eloise. She reverse-engineers her: geolocates the kitchen from a reflection in a kettle, times her posting schedule to her cycle, notices the day the wine bottles moved from recycling to trash, catalogs the bruise the ring light was angled to hide. When Eloise posts—raw, no makeup, at 3 a.m.—that she's "finally hiring someone to help me hold it together," Wren already knows the neighborhood, the gate code pattern, the brand of tea. She applies as a stranger. She gets the job. And living inside the real house, Wren discovers the woman behind the feed is warmer, sadder, and far more dangerous than the screen let on—because Eloise's content has always been bait, engineered by an algorithm Eloise doesn't fully control, tuned to surface the followers most likely to do something. Wren came to save Eloise. Eloise's feed selected Wren to be used. Told entirely in Wren's opaque, obsessive first person—she withholds the reason she can afford to quit her job to work for near-nothing, and why she flinches at police—the novel collapses the safe distance between the watcher and the watched until neither Wren nor the reader can tell who is grooming whom.
concept.obsession_engine: The obsession is parasocial intimacy weaponized into forensic knowledge. Wren's job—annotating human suffering for an AI—has trained her to see people as data to be labeled, and she turns that skill on Eloise, "knowing" her more completely than any friend does while never having heard her voice unfiltered. The engine drives every beat: the fantasy of being known back, the withdrawal when Eloise goes offline, the escalating need to close the gap between the curated feed and the real body in the real house. It generates dread because Wren's love is indistinguishable from surveillance, and tenderness from control. Toxic intimacy runs both directions—the reader feels the seductive pull of being finally, totally seen by another person, then watches that same longing become the exact vulnerability the algorithm was mining. "I shouldn't want to be this close to her" is the heartbeat.
concept.protagonist_engine: Wren is the reader's own worst 2 a.m. scrolling habit given a body and a purpose. We root for her because her loneliness is legible and modern—she's not a monster, she's someone who found the only human she felt real next to on a screen. Her competence is thrilling: she notices what we'd miss, and being inside her pattern-matching mind is a genuine pleasure. She's morally grey because her devotion is real AND her methods are violations, and she narrates the violations as love, so the reader is complicit in reframing them. She's psychologically opaque, not lying—she genuinely can't see her own darkness, withholds her past (the earlier person she "helped," the reason she flinches at police) not to trick us but because she's protecting herself from her own memory. That's what makes her repeatable across a series: the obsessive-outsider-who-inserts-herself is a renewable character type, a new one each book.
concept.antagonist_engine: Layered, so the "villain" keeps reassigning. Surface antagonist: Eloise herself—magnetic, manipulative, possibly complicit in her own trap, the object of obsession who turns out to have her own agenda. Deeper antagonist: the recommendation algorithm and the shadowy growth-hacking manager, Dane, who runs Eloise's brand and has quietly A/B-tested her most vulnerable content to surface "high-parasocial-risk" followers—people primed to show up in the real world—because devoted obsessives are the most monetizable and, when needed, the most disposable. Deepest antagonist: the truth about Wren herself. The opposing force she can't defeat is her own pattern—she has done a version of this before, and the story she tells about being a rescuer is the thing standing between her and what she actually is.
concept.central_threat: Someone connected to Eloise's account has already died—a prior "helper" who moved into the house eight months ago and is now a deleted highlight reel and a missing-person post Eloise pinned then quietly archived. Wren occupies the dead woman's old room, wears a version of her role, and is being fed the same curated intimacy that preceded that death. The threat engine: is Eloise a victim being harvested, a predator selecting her next disposable devotee, or bait in a trap neither woman controls—and which role is Wren walking into? The clock is Eloise's next "big vulnerable drop," which the algorithm is spinning up to surface the next Wren.
concept.stakes: Personal: Wren has staked her last money, her fragile stability, and her one experience of feeling seen on being real to Eloise; if it's all algorithmic bait, the one true thing in her life is a targeting parameter. Survival: the previous helper died and Wren is standing in her footprints. Moral: to save Eloise—or herself—Wren may have to do the exact violent thing her own buried past proves she's capable of, confirming she was never the rescuer. Public/thematic: if Wren exposes the harvest, she detonates her own anonymity and the reason she can never talk to police.
concept.escalation_pattern: Act 1 (screen): Wren's remote surveillance of Eloise, the interview, getting hired—tension from proximity and near-misses of being recognized as a superfan. Chapter hooks land on things Wren already knows that she shouldn't. Act 2a (house): living inside the real Eloise, intimacy deepening, small wrongnesses accumulating—the archived helper, the locked studio, Dane's metrics dashboard, the wine, the second phone. Every comfort curdles into evidence. Act 2b (collapse of distance): Wren and Eloise's relationship turns genuinely intimate and genuinely predatory at once; Wren realizes she was selected, and simultaneously the reader realizes Wren has withheld what she is. Midpoint flip: the watcher was watched—Eloise (or Dane) has a folder on Wren. Act 3 (real world): the next drop goes live, the trap springs, and Wren must choose whether to become the weapon the algorithm always predicted she'd be. Escalation moves screen -> house -> body, and knowledge -> intimacy -> violence.
concept.twist_architecture: Twin earned twists. TWIST ONE (external): Eloise is not the sole author of her own trap—the algorithm and Dane have been optimizing her vulnerability content to surface obsessive followers, and the previous helper's death was the system working as designed; Eloise half-knows and hates herself for it. Planted clues: engagement graphs Wren glimpses that spike on Eloise's rawest posts; Dane's phrase "high-intent audience"; posts that seem tailored to Wren specifically (because they're tailored to her exact profile cluster); the archived missing helper; the second, unmonetized private account where Eloise posts the real truth to no one. TWIST TWO (the opaque-narrator twist, the real one): Wren is not a first-time obsessive who drifted into this—she is a repeat actor. The reason she can quit her job, flinch at police, and read a curated-vulnerability trap so fluently is that she was the previous helper's "friend from online" too; she inserted herself into a stranger's life once before, it ended in a death she narrates around, and the annotation job is penance-adjacent, not origin. She didn't stumble into the algorithm's harvest—her pattern is why the algorithm flagged her as high-intent. She is exactly the disposable devotee the system selects AND the predator it can aim. This is earned by her consistent withholding (never explaining her savings, her fluency, her flinch), by "rescuer" language that overcorrects, and by a recurring sensory memory she refuses to finish—not a lie retracted in chapter forty, but a truth the reader can assemble from what she can't bear to say. Shocking-but-logical: on reread, every act of "love" was recognizable behavior.
concept.series_engine: Series brand: "Feed" — connected standalones, one per book, each a different obsessive narrator whose parasocial fixation collapses into the real world, each a fresh voice, victim, and platform (wellness influencer, true-crime podcaster, a dead creator's still-posting account, a rising musician, an AI companion chatbot). No recurring couple. The thin connective tissue is the platform itself and the recommendation system—the algorithm as a recurring, evolving off-page character, with occasional shared minor figures (Dane's growth agency; the moderation-annotation underworld) that reward returning readers without gating new ones. The repeatable engine: take a stranger everyone thinks they know from a screen, hand the narration to the one follower who knows too much, and collapse the distance. Book 1 fully resolves Wren and Eloise; the algorithm survives, guaranteeing the next harvest.
concept.why_buy_book_one: It's the most phone-native version of the obsession thriller on the shelf: everyone has felt parasocially close to a stranger online, everyone has been unnerved by an algorithm that knows them too well, and this book turns that universal 2 a.m. feeling into a knife. The hook is instantly legible, the narrator is a magnetic morally-grey mind you can't look away from, the twist reframes the whole book on reread, and the voltage—finally being SEEN by someone, and that being the trap—delivers dark-romance emotional intensity with zero genre-bending. It reads like You crossed with The Silent Patient for the TikTok era.
concept.why_continue: Readers finish Book 1 having fallen for a voice, then learn the real monster is the system that keeps selecting people like Wren—so the promise of Book 2 is another irresistible obsessive, another stranger-you-think-you-know, another collapse, powered by the same off-page algorithm that just proved it will never stop. The brand promise ("a new obsession every book, same dread") is exactly the bingeable, rapid-release engine that built McFadden and Kepnes, delivered on author brand rather than a single recurring couple.
concept.booktok_hook: "She memorized every frame of a stranger's life online, then got hired into her house. She thought she was there to save her. The algorithm chose her to do something else."
concept.buildability_notes: Extremely solo-indie friendly. Contemporary, phone-native setting—one primary location (Eloise's house) plus screens; no procedural, medical, legal, or period research burden. The "expertise" (content annotation, engagement metrics, influencer growth-hacking, basic OSINT like reflection-geolocation and posting-pattern analysis) is googleable in a weekend and adds authentic texture cheaply. No firearms/forensics gatekeeping. Single close-first-person POV keeps drafting fast. Structure is built for bingeability: ~85-90k words, 60-70 short chapters, each ending on a Wren-knows-too-much or wrongness-in-the-house hook; screen-content interstitials (captions, DMs, comment threads, annotation logs) break up pages and boost pace. Format is rapid-release native because each series entry is a clean standalone reusing the same world bible. Cover design is easy and franchise-able: a face half-lit by phone glow / a ring light / an eye reflected in a screen, with a consistent series treatment.
concept.originality_claim: Most obsession thrillers put the obsessive OUTSIDE looking in (You) or the manipulator on a pedestal (Verity). This one fuses the parasocial-watcher premise with the ALGORITHM as active procurer: the feed isn't just how she watches, it's a predatory system that curated the victim's vulnerability specifically to surface and select obsessives like the narrator—making the reader complicit in the exact behavioral profiling that targets them daily. It weaponizes the reader's own recommendation-feed unease. And it subverts the rescuer narrator: the twist isn't "she lied," it's that her fluency at reading the trap is itself the evidence she's done this before—the harvested devotee and the aimed predator are the same person. Nobody's positioned "the algorithm chose the stalker" as the engine, and no affluent-suburb-marriage template in sight.

concept.logline: A woman convinces her entire neighborhood — and eventually the police — that the reclusive man across the street is stalking her, and we follow her flawless, sympathetic account of her own terror right up until we realize she chose him, she engineered every "incident," and she is grooming a scapegoat for something she has already done.
concept.premise: Nadia Voss, thirty-four, moves into a modest rental on Alder Court in a tired Ohio commuter suburb and tells us, in warm, self-deprecating, utterly credible first person, that the man in the house opposite — Emmett Boyle, a widowed night-shift EMS dispatcher who never quite meets anyone's eyes — has begun watching her. First it's small: his porch light angled at her bedroom, a figure at the tree line, footprints in the flowerbed under her window. She does everything right. She keeps a log. She photographs the prints. She befriends the neighbors — the retired teacher next door, the young couple with the doorbell cam, the HOA busybody — and lets them draw their own alarmed conclusions. She is careful never to overstate. She is the perfect victim: measured, reluctant to accuse, apologetic for the fuss. The reader's protective instinct locks onto her by chapter three. But the narration is doing something subtler than lying. Nadia never tells us a single provable falsehood. She simply withholds the frame around each true fact — and the reader, primed to see a woman in danger, assembles the wrong picture from real pieces. What she is actually building is a case: a documented, witnessed, timestamped narrative of a fixated man escalating toward violence, so that when Emmett Boyle is found dead and it looks like a confrontation gone wrong, the entire street will already believe he was the predator and she was the prey. The horror isn't that she's imagining a stalker. It's that she picked him — a grieving, isolated man with no one to vouch for him — the way a spider picks a corner. And the twist under the twist is why: Emmett isn't random. Eleven months ago his ambulance dispatch decision, a triage call under load, left Nadia's daughter waiting forty minutes on a roadside. This whole architecture is a revenge she has spent a year designing to look like self-defense.
concept.obsession_engine: The obsession is Nadia's — cold, patient, total — but it wears the mask of his. The book's engine is a fixation running backward: the reader is told a story of a man obsessed with a woman, while experiencing a woman obsessed with a man, and the toxic-intimacy voltage comes from how intimately she has studied Emmett. She knows his shift patterns, his dead wife's name, the brand of tea he buys, the exact angle of his grief. She has watched him for a year with the attentiveness of a lover and the intent of a predator. The 'I shouldn't want this' charge is displaced into 'I shouldn't be rooting for her' — and into the genuinely unsettling tenderness she feels toward her victim, a closeness that reads as attraction until you understand it as appraisal. Every chapter tightens the noose while the prose stays gentle, reasonable, sympathetic. The obsession drives plot because each 'incident' she stages must be more provable and more public than the last, escalating not toward a confrontation she fears but toward one she is scheduling.
concept.protagonist_engine: Nadia Voss is the monster in the good-guy costume — and she's repeatable because her costume is the most trusted archetype in the genre: the careful, credible woman being disbelieved. We root for her despite ourselves for three reasons the text engineers. First, competence: she is brilliant, controlled, and always ten moves ahead, and readers reflexively love a mastermind narrating her own machine. Second, the withholding is generous, not sneering — she never mocks the reader, she confides, so the betrayal when it lands feels like something we did to ourselves, not something she did to us. Third, and load-bearing: the grief underneath is real. Her daughter is really dead. Her account of that loss, dropped in fragments, is the one place the controlled voice cracks, and it earns her just enough sympathy that the reader keeps rationalizing. She is 'psychologically opaque' in the evolved sense — we get full access to her surface reasoning and zero access to her intent, so re-reading reveals every sentence was true and every sentence was a trap. Her repeatable engine for a series: a protagonist who constructs perfect victim-narratives to destroy the person she has judged guilty, each book a new target, a new town, a new borrowed archetype.
concept.antagonist_engine: The opposing force is Emmett Boyle himself — but inverted into the genre's most disturbing shape: the antagonist is the innocent. He is the obstacle not because he threatens her but because he keeps failing to be the villain she needs. He is kind to her. He shovels her walk. He notices she seems frightened and, catastrophically for himself, tries to help — which she photographs and reframes as approach. His decency is the tension: every genuinely good thing he does, she must convert into evidence, and the reader watches a real man be slowly authored into a criminal. The secondary antagonist is Detective Ruth Okafor, methodical and quietly unconvinced, who represents the truth pressing back — she notices the log is too clean, the timeline too tidy, the victim too composed. The deepest antagonist is the truth itself: the fact of what Nadia has done to an innocent man, which the narration is structured to keep even Nadia from fully looking at until the end.
concept.central_threat: On the surface: a fixated man escalating toward a woman living alone — the danger the whole neighborhood believes in. Underneath: the real threat is Nadia, and the engine of dread is dramatic irony in slow reverse — the reader gradually realizes the person in mortal danger is the 'stalker,' and that we have been cheering the countdown to an innocent man's death. The secret at the core is twofold: that Nadia manufactured every incident, and that Emmett was chosen for a reason connected to her daughter's death — meaning this was never fear, it was a year-long execution disguised as self-defense.
concept.stakes: For Nadia: getting away with it — a killing she has engineered to be not just unpunished but publicly mourned as justified, with herself recast as survivor. For Emmett: his life, and worse, his name, which she destroys before she destroys him. For Detective Okafor: whether the truth can outrun a narrative that a whole community has already emotionally invested in. For the reader personally: the stakes are their own complicity — the book is built so that finishing it means having wanted an innocent man caught, which is the emotional payload that makes people press it into a friend's hands.
concept.escalation_pattern: Act one (the sympathetic build): small, deniable, private incidents — the log begins, the neighbors are seeded, the reader bonds with Nadia and fears for her; ends on the first witnessed incident, corroborated by the doorbell cam, that makes it 'real.' Act two (the public case): incidents escalate in provability and audience — HOA meeting, a police report, a restraining-order petition — while cracks appear: a timestamp that's too convenient, a moment of tenderness toward Emmett that doesn't fit fear, a fragment of the dead daughter surfacing. Detective Okafor starts pulling threads. Midpoint gut-punch: the reader catches Nadia in a withholding, not a lie — she 'forgot' to mention she was on Emmett's street before she ever rented on it. Act three (the reveal and the reversal of sympathy): the staged final confrontation, Emmett's death made to look like self-defense, then Okafor's reconstruction detonating the true frame — the daughter, the dispatch call, the year of planning — while Nadia's controlled voice finally tells us, calmly, that she'd do it again. Tension escalates by moving the horror from 'will he hurt her' to 'what has she done' to 'what did we want.'
concept.twist_architecture: Primary twist: the victim is the predator — Nadia staged everything. Earned by clues planted in plain sight: she photographs footprints before any 'sighting' could have alerted her; her log's handwriting is consistent across weeks of supposedly escalating panic; she knows details about Emmett (his wife's name, his schedule) she was never told; she 'happens' to befriend exactly the neighbors with cameras and standing to testify; she flinches at the word 'ambulance' twice before we know why. Under-twist (the motive): Emmett wasn't random — the reveal that his triage decision left her daughter dying on a roadside recontextualizes every tender, studying glance as the patience of revenge; planted by her fragmentary grief, a redacted news clipping she keeps, and a scanner app on her phone we're shown early and dismiss. Final micro-twist (the opacity payoff): Okafor proves the manufacture but can't prove premeditation — and Nadia's last narration, still gentle, still reasonable, lets the reader alone see she planned all of it, making us the sole witness to a confession no court will hear. Shocking but logical: on reread, every fact was true; only the frame was withheld, satisfying the 'no last-chapter liar' rule.
concept.series_engine: Book one is a complete standalone: the crime, the reveal, and Nadia's reckoning with Okafor all close. The repeatable engine is Nadia Voss as a recurring architect of 'perfect victim' narratives — a woman who appoints herself judge of people the system failed to punish, then constructs an unimpeachable innocence around their destruction. Each book: a new town, a new borrowed archetype (the frightened tenant, the bullied coworker, the harassed patient), a new target she has judged guilty of something real, and a new detective-shaped truth pressing back. The series brand is author-level, not couple-level: 'the woman you'll root for and shouldn't, again.' Connected-standalone scalability comes from Okafor as an optional through-line — a detective who suspects a pattern she can never quite prove — turning the series into a slow cat-and-mouse across books without requiring any single one to be unfinished. Rapid-release friendly because the template is portable and the research burden stays low.
concept.why_buy_book_one: Because the hook is a dare — 'she's the victim, and she's lying to you about being the victim, and you'll love her anyway' — and because it takes the most familiar setup in domestic thriller (a woman convinced no one believes her about her stalker) and detonates it from the inside. The reader gets the comfort of a genre they know and the shock of having that genre used against them. It delivers dark-romance voltage (obsession, forbidden rooting-interest, unbearable intimacy between hunter and prey) with pure thriller mechanics and a real, earned, rereadable twist — no safe ending, no cheat.
concept.why_continue: Because book one converts the reader into an accomplice and then asks the question that guarantees book two: who else has she decided is guilty? Nadia is the rare narrator you finish the book actively distrusting and still wanting more of — you need to watch the trick work again, on someone new, now that you know the mechanism, to see if she can fool you a second time when you're looking for it. The Okafor thread adds the pull of an unfinished hunt. Readers who love McFadden's bingeability get a mastermind they can follow across a shelf.
concept.booktok_hook: "She's telling you she's the victim. Every word is true. She's the predator. And by page 200 you'll be rooting for her to get away with it."
concept.buildability_notes: Extremely low research and gatekeeper burden. Single contemporary suburban setting (one street, a handful of houses), tiny cast, no procedural-heavy forensics required — the police presence is one detective and can be kept lean and character-driven. The 'evidence' is domestic and accessible: doorbell cams, HOA emails, a personal log, phone photos, a scanner app — all things a solo author can render convincingly without expert consultants. The one research node (EMS dispatch triage) is a single, googleable, emotionally-loaded detail, not a technical spine. Voice-driven first person keeps production fast; short chapters end naturally on the reframe-hooks the structure already generates. Target 85-90k, easily rapid-release given the portable per-book template. The only craft discipline required — and it's the whole game — is rigorous 'withhold the frame, never state a falsehood' plotting so the reread holds; that's a plotting-spreadsheet problem, not a research problem, and it's fully controllable by a disciplined solo author.
concept.originality_claim: The comps all reveal a liar (Amy in Gone Girl confesses her construction; the narrators of You and The Silent Patient hide guilt behind a lying voice). This concept never lies — it weaponizes the reader's own genre literacy, using the 'disbelieved woman' archetype as the murder weapon, so the betrayal is self-inflicted and the reread proves every sentence honest. It also inverts the target of sympathy at the structural level: not a victim who turns out to be complicated, but a predator wearing the victim archetype so completely that rooting for her IS the trap. Fresh vs. the affluent-marriage template because it abandons the couple entirely — the toxic intimacy is between a killer and the innocent stranger she has chosen — and fresh vs. dark romance because the intimacy resolves in execution, not union, with the twist (not a HEA) as the binding promise.

concept.logline: A woman who trains AI "griefbots" — conversational ghosts built from the dead's voice memos and texts — becomes obsessed with one client's beautiful life and starts stepping into it, seducing the grieving widower and becoming his dead wife, only to discover he requested her by name because replacing dead women is exactly what he does — and she's next in the archive.
concept.premise: In rainy Portland, Wren Ash works for Afterword, a grief-tech startup that resurrects the dead as chat avatars: bereaved families upload years of a loved one's voicemails, texts, and videos, and Wren — a "memory engineer" — trains the model until the ghost sounds real enough to text goodnight. Wren is very good at her job because she has always been good at disappearing into other people. Assigned the account of Iris Hale — a luminous, adored ceramicist killed in a hillside car crash — Wren spends her nights immersed in the dead woman's voice: her jokes, her marriage, her friendships, the exact cadence of being loved. Iris had everything Wren has spent her life pressing her face against the glass to see. So Wren starts to borrow it. She wears Iris's discontinued perfume. She "runs into" Iris's widower, Julian, an acoustic architect who builds silent, sound-dead rooms for the ultra-rich. She learns Iris's friends, Iris's laugh, Iris's opinions on wine. She lets Julian mistake the ache in her for grief, and lets herself be pulled into his bed and his beautiful mid-century house in the West Hills, until she is no longer training the ghost of Iris Hale — she is auditioning to replace her. But the griefbot keeps saying things Wren never fed it. Iris's last voice memos hold a second voice, and fear. And Julian, it turns out, did not find Wren by accident. He asked for her. Because there was a woman before Iris. And the archive is always hungry for one more.
concept.obsession_engine: The obsession is grief-as-envy inverted into grief-as-appetite: Wren isn't mourning Iris — she covets being Iris, because Iris was demonstrably, archivally worth grieving, and no one has ever wanted to keep Wren. The griefbot is the perfect toxic-intimacy device: Wren falls in love with a person she is literally building, night after night, a dead woman who "answers" her, who is warm and available and never leaves — the most one-sided, controllable intimacy imaginable, until it starts answering back with things it shouldn't know. Every chapter is intercut with Afterword chat transcripts, so the reader watches Wren flirt with, confide in, and slowly become the ghost. The engine drives the plot because each step deeper into Iris's data unlocks the next intrusion into Iris's real life (the friends, the house, the bed, the estate) — replacement is the through-line, and the closer Wren gets to being loved as Iris, the closer she gets to Iris's actual fate.
concept.protagonist_engine: Wren Ash is a psychologically opaque, first-person narrator who never once tells the reader a clean lie — she withholds, reframes, and skips. She narrates her intrusions as tenderness ("I only wanted to give him his wife back"), rationalizes theft as caretaking, and glides past her own history so smoothly you don't notice the holes until they bleed: she never says her surname out loud, flinches from cameras, can forge a signature from three samples, and knows precisely how a person legally becomes someone else. We root for her because she is the unloved outsider with her nose to the glass — working-class, invisible, aching to be the kind of woman someone would spend a fortune resurrecting — and because the deeper she sinks, the more clearly SHE is the one being hunted. She's repeatable as a series engine: an identity-absorber, a woman who steps into vacated lives, so the "brand" can drop her — or another absorber like her — into a new dead person's world each book.
concept.antagonist_engine: Julian Hale is the opposing force and the mirror: a soft-spoken acoustic architect who builds anechoic chambers — rooms engineered to be perfectly, unbearably silent — and who, it emerges, has been building the same thing out of women. He is a curator of grief. He does not simply lose wives; he perfects them by ending them at their most beloved moment and preserving them forever as archives he can revisit, edit, and control. He chose Wren, requested her from Afterword by name, because he recognized in her the one thing Iris finally refused to be: infinitely moldable. Julian is the obsessive Wren is meant not to notice she resembles — he replaces the dead the way she does, only he creates the vacancy first. The truth itself is the secondary antagonist: Iris's real voice, buried in the unedited data, fighting to be heard over the sweetened ghost Julian curated.
concept.central_threat: Three braided dangers: (1) the mystery of how Iris actually died and what the griefbot's impossible, un-trained answers are trying to tell Wren; (2) Julian's pattern — a previous "wife," Delphine, whose voice is sealed in the anechoic room, and the dawning certainty that Wren is being shaped into the next preserved entry; and (3) Wren's own buried past, the reason she is uniquely, dangerously qualified to vanish into a dead woman — a secret that, if it surfaces, destroys the borrowed life she's living right now.
concept.stakes: Wren's life, literally — she is being groomed into a corpse-shaped role, and completing the transformation into Iris means completing Iris's fate. But the deeper stakes are the ones she'd die before admitting: her one shot at being loved absolutely, at being someone worth keeping, is the same trap designed to erase her. To survive she has to give up the fantasy that got her here; to keep the fantasy she has to walk into the silent room. And her secret past means she can't call the police, can't be looked at too closely, can't prove she is anyone at all.
concept.escalation_pattern: Act 1 — INTIMACY: Wren trains Iris's ghost and lets it colonize her; small trespasses (the perfume, the memorial, one "accidental" meeting with Julian) each end on a hook. Act 2A — REPLACEMENT: Wren wins Julian, the friends, the house; every acquired piece of Iris's life raises the reward and the wrongness. Turn: the griefbot answers with a fact Wren never trained — the data has been edited by someone else. Act 2B — SUSPICION: Wren, hunting proof that Iris was murdered, finds Delphine instead — the woman before — and realizes Julian requested her on purpose; the hunter becomes the hunted. Midpoint gut-punch: he isn't grieving his wife, he's rehearsing his next one. Act 3 — INVERSION: cornered, Wren's own secret detonates (she is already someone she stole), which is exactly the weapon that lets her out-replace the replacer. Climax in the anechoic chamber, in total silence, where the only witness is the griefbot itself. Each act tightens the noose from seduction to survival.
concept.twist_architecture: Layered, mirror-structured, and planted throughout. TWIST 1 (midpoint): Iris didn't die by accident — the griefbot's un-trainable answers, the gaps in the "sweetened" data, and Iris's final voice memos (a second voice; fear) reveal Julian curated her into a ghost by ending her. Clues: the model surfaces details Wren never fed it (someone else has been inside the data); a locked "legacy" file; Iris's laugh that stops mid-memo. TWIST 2 (three-quarter): Julian chose Wren. He didn't hire Afterword and get her by chance — he named her, because there was a Delphine before Iris, and he collects. Clues: his too-precise knowledge of Wren's schedule; a woman's voice already sealed in the silent room; his habit of "improving" the dead. Wren, the predator, is prey. TWIST 3 (the opaque-narrator reveal, earned not confessed): Wren was never Wren. Years ago she let another woman die and stepped into her name and life — which is why she is peerless at becoming someone, why she recognized Julian's game too late, and why she can't go to the police. This recontextualizes every withhold in the book (no surname, no photos, the forgery skill) as clues, not cheats. RESOLUTION: the one person alive who knows exactly how to disappear into a dead woman uses it — she becomes Iris so completely she seizes Iris's estate, accounts, and the griefbot, and traps Julian inside his own archive, letting the ghost he built deliver the last word. Shocking, but every beat is on the page beforehand.
concept.series_engine: Book 1 is a complete standalone: Wren's arc closes, Julian is dealt with, the twist lands. The repeatable engine is AUTHOR-BRAND / SHARED-WORLD, not one recurring couple — "the Afterword novels," a connected-standalone line set in the world of grief-tech and stolen lives, each book a different obsessive stepping into a different vacated identity, loosely linked by the Afterword company, a recurring detective-of-the-dead who audits suspicious griefbots, or Wren herself resurfacing under yet another name. The premise generates infinite fresh domestic-uncanny hooks (a nanny becomes the dead mother; a twin trains her twin's ghost; a griefbot that won't stop accusing its family) while keeping a consistent brand promise: obsession, replacement, and a twist you didn't earn the right to see coming.
concept.why_buy_book_one: It weaponizes a 2026-current fear — AI ghosts of the dead — into pure emotional voltage, then delivers a triple-locked twist that reframes the whole book. The griefbot-transcript interstitials are irresistible and screenshot-native for BookTok. And it scratches the exact "root for someone you shouldn't" itch of You and Verity while promising a real, un-telegraphed detonation and no guaranteed happy ending — the dark-romance ache with a thriller's teeth.
concept.why_continue: The final pages leave Wren having survived by becoming someone else again — richer, freer, and more erased than ever, a woman who has now proven she can step into any vacated life and walk out clean. That's an engine, not a loose end: readers will want to watch her (or the next absorber like her) do it again, in a new house, with a new ghost, and a new person who thinks they found her by accident. The shared world guarantees the same brand promise every time — obsession, replacement, earned twist — with a wholly new domestic nightmare.
concept.booktok_hook: "She trains AI ghosts of the dead. Then she fell for a grieving widower — and started becoming his dead wife. She didn't know he was training her too. And the archive always needs one more."
concept.buildability_notes: Fully solo-buildable. Contemporary Portland; single first-person POV; ~85–90k; short chapters ending on hooks, intercut with griefbot chat transcripts (fast to write, high-hook, BookTok-native). Research burden is minimal and low-gatekeeper: grief-tech is speculative-light (no procedural, legal, or forensic accuracy demands), and the acoustic-architecture / anechoic-chamber detail is atmospheric flavor requiring only surface reading. No cops, courtrooms, or expert-verifiable jargon to trip on. Cover is instantly designable: a woman's face dissolving into audio waveform / static, or two overlapping women, cool desaturated blue-grey — classic domestic-thriller shelf signal. Rapid-release friendly via the connected-standalone structure.
concept.originality_claim: Comps mediate obsession through a stalker's diary (You), a hidden manuscript (Verity), or a diagnosis (The Silent Patient); here the intimacy device is an AI resurrection of the dead the narrator is literally building and falling in love with — fresh, current, and uniquely creepy. The structural novelty is the MIRROR twist: this isn't just an obsessive who replaces a dead woman, it's a replacer who discovers she's being replaced by a fellow replacer — "the predator is the prey," with the opaque-narrator reveal (she's already living a stolen identity) recontextualizing her skill instead of cheating the reader with a last-page confession. Grief-replacement plus AI griefbots plus double-replacement is a combination the current market hasn't shelved.

concept.logline: A memory-clinic technician and her most damaged patient begin secretly rewriting each other's recollections of the night a man died between them—until neither can tell which of them is the killer, the confessor, or the one being erased.
concept.premise:
Wren Ostrander, 31, runs the overnight suite at a boutique "memory reconsolidation" clinic in coastal Astoria, Oregon—a legal, lightly-regulated therapy where clients revisit a traumatic memory under a mild sedative while a technician talks them through it, and the memory softens each time it's recalled. Wren is exceptional at it because she is a practiced forgetter herself: she has a hole in her own past she has spent a decade refusing to look at.

Then Silas Roeck books a course of sessions. He's there to dull a single memory: standing over the body of a man named Daniel Voss at the bottom of a stairwell, eight months ago, an unsolved death ruled accidental. But in his first session Silas describes the stairwell, the rain, the green coat on the dead man—and Wren realizes she remembers that coat. She was there. Her missing night and his unbearable one are the same night.

Neither says so at first. Instead they begin an off-the-books arrangement: he keeps coming, she keeps "treating" him, and in the dark of the suite they start trading pieces of the night to reconstruct what happened—except the therapy that's supposed to soften memory can also be used to plant it. Every session, one of them nudges the other's version a half-inch: toward guilt, toward innocence, toward each other. They fall into a suffocating intimacy built entirely on shared culpability—the only person who understands the worst night of your life is the one who might have caused it. Wren narrates, and she is honestly, structurally unreliable: she is missing the same hours the reader needs, and she is emotionally incapable of believing Silas is using her even as the evidence stacks up. The reader watches two people edit each other in real time and cannot tell who picked up the pen first.
concept.obsession_engine: The obsession is EPISTEMIC and MUTUAL: each is the sole keeper of the other's missing self. Wren needs Silas because he holds the eight hours she erased; Silas needs Wren because her clinical skill can make his guilt bearable—or make it disappear. That dependency curdles into toxic intimacy: they meet nightly in a dark room, sedative-loose, confessing things they'd never say sober, and every confession is also a move. The escalation mechanism is built into the premise—the reconsolidation therapy literally degrades the source memory each time it's recalled, so the more they revisit the night together, the less either of them can independently verify it. They are metabolizing the truth by touching it. The reader feels the "I shouldn't want this" voltage because the closeness is real AND it is the exact instrument of harm: to be understood by this person is to be edited by them. Neither can leave, because leaving means never knowing who they are.
concept.protagonist_engine: Wren Ostrander: a professional eraser of pain who has weaponized forgetting on herself, so she's the rare narrator who is unreliable in good faith—she isn't hiding the truth from us, she has hidden it from herself and we're trapped behind her scar tissue. We root for her because her tenderness is genuine: she got into this work to spare people the thing she couldn't spare herself, and watching her fall for the one man she should quarantine is watching a caretaker finally want care back. She's morally grey because she keeps making the small, defensible choice—one more off-book session, one more thing she won't report—and each is a step deeper into complicity. She's repeatable as a series lead-type (the "professional forgetter" / caretaker-with-a-locked-room-in-her-past) without being the same person twice. Her signature is a chilling competence: she can talk anyone through the worst moment of their life in a voice like warm water, and we slowly realize that voice is also a scalpel.
concept.antagonist_engine: There is no external villain—the antagonist is the OTHER OBSESSIVE and the TRUTH itself, fused. Silas Roeck functions as antagonist, love object, and mirror simultaneously: a man who may be a grieving accidental witness, a calculating manipulator who chose Wren's clinic on purpose, or a fellow victim being edited by her. The engine of opposition is that the reader (and Wren) can never fix which. Every warm scene is evidence he's real; every warm scene is evidence he's working her. The secondary adversarial force is Daniel Voss, the dead man, whose true relationship to Wren is the locked room—his death is the gravity both of them orbit. The genius of the folie à deux is that the "manipulator" and the "manipulated" swap places chapter to chapter, and the final antagonist turns out to be the shared, jointly-authored false memory they built together, which now stands between them and a truth neither can survive alone.
concept.central_threat: Two intertwined engines. MYSTERY: what actually happened on the stairwell the night Daniel Voss died, and what is Wren's real connection to him? SECRET/DANGER: the reconsolidation sessions are actively destroying the only evidence—their own memories—so there is a ticking clock on truth (soon neither will have an uncorrupted recollection left), plus a live external threat: Voss's death is being quietly reopened, and a detective's inquiries mean whichever of them the false memory finally frames as the killer will go down for it. The knife's edge: the very intimacy keeping them alive is the process erasing their alibi.
concept.stakes: Personal: Wren's sanity and identity—if she recovers the night, she may learn she killed a man; if she doesn't, she stays a stranger to herself forever and lets Silas author her. Legal/survival: the reopened investigation means one of them is going to be charged, and the reconsolidation has corrupted their memories so badly that neither can prove innocence—the loser is whoever the joint false narrative fingers. Emotional: the only love either has ever felt understood by is inseparable from the person most able to destroy them. To save herself Wren may have to prove the man she loves is a killer—or accept that she is.
concept.escalation_pattern: ACT 1 (curiosity → complicity): Recognition of the shared night; the secret off-book sessions begin; tender, addictive, deniable. Hook = the green coat. ACT 2A (folie à deux deepens): Their versions start converging in ways that are too clean—each realizes the other might be editing them, but the intimacy is now a need. Small betrayals: a planted detail, a session that goes further than agreed. ACT 2B (the mirror cracks): The investigation reopens; a piece of hard external evidence (a parking fob, a text, a second witness) contradicts BOTH their memories, proving at least one version was manufactured—but not by whom. Paranoia detonates the intimacy while making it more compulsive. ACT 3 (authorship revealed): The truth of Voss and of Wren's locked night surfaces, recontextualizing every prior session—then a final turn about who initiated the whole arrangement. Each act tightens by removing a source of external verification, forcing the reader deeper into two unreliable heads with nothing solid to stand on—until the last chapter gives them the one solid thing, and it's worse than not knowing.
concept.twist_architecture: PRIMARY TWIST (earned, mid-late): Silas did not stumble into Wren's clinic—he sought her out because he already knew who she was on the stairwell night. He is Daniel Voss's brother. He came to use the one therapy that could make a killer confess without a lawyer present, recording the sessions to build a case. Planted clues: he always books the same overnight slot (control of setting); he "remembers" details of the clinic's protocol a first-timer wouldn't; he never lets Wren treat HIS memory as deeply as he probes hers; an early throwaway line about a brother "back east" that Wren accepts without follow-up. SECOND TWIST (the mirror flips): but the reconsolidation genuinely worked on HIM too—in trying to extract Wren's guilt, Silas has been overwriting his own memory of that night, and the version he's built to convict her has quietly rewritten what he knows, until he can no longer be sure his brother's death wasn't his own doing, deflected onto her. The hunter edited himself. THIRD/FINAL EARNED TWIST: the locked room—Wren's missing hours—resolves not to "Wren killed Daniel" but to "Wren was the one who pulled Daniel off Silas at the top of the stairs; the fall was the brothers' fight, and Wren, a stranger who intervened, blacked out from the head injury she took and has spent a year unable to face that she failed to save him." She isn't the killer OR the innocent bystander—she's the witness both brothers needed to erase for opposite reasons: Silas to survive his guilt, Wren to survive her helplessness. The false memory they jointly built was two people trying to make a meaningless tragedy have an author. Clues seeded from page one: Wren's own untreated scar/headache she never explains; her compulsive need to have caused things (survivor's logic); the green coat detail she "remembers" is the one thing she couldn't have seen from where a killer would stand—but exactly what a rescuer reaching down would see. Shocking, but every piece is on the page.
concept.series_engine: Book 1 is a complete, standalone tragedy-thriller with a closed central mystery. The repeatable engine is the CLINIC and the PREMISE-ENGINE, not the couple: "The Reconsolidation Files" (working series brand)—each book takes a different overnight technician or client at a memory-reconsolidation clinic (this one, or a sister franchise location in a new city), where someone is using the therapy to edit a shared crime, and the narrator is a differently-broken obsessive tangled with a differently-dangerous mirror. Connected-standalone structure: Wren can recur as a minor thread (she now knows the clinic's power is a loaded gun) or vanish entirely; the through-line is the seductive, weaponizable technology of forgetting and the toxic intimacies it breeds. Author brand = "domestic thrillers where love is the murder weapon and memory is the crime scene." Endlessly scalable, zero recurring-couple fatigue, each book a fresh two-hander.
concept.why_buy_book_one: It fuses the year's hottest voltage (obsession, toxic intimacy, "I shouldn't want him") with a genuinely fresh, high-concept thriller engine—two unreliable narrators literally editing each other's memory of the same death—so the unreliability is baked into the PLOT rather than being a last-chapter cheat. Readers get the You/Verity emotional intensity and the Silent Patient twist-payload, but with a mechanism no one's seen: a therapy that makes forgetting a weapon of intimacy. The green-coat hook is instantly repeatable; the setting is a single dim room requiring near-zero research; the moral question ("would you let the only person who understands you rewrite what you did?") is tailor-made for BookTok debate and duet reactions.
concept.why_continue: The premise engine is a machine for fresh, self-contained obsession thrillers: the clinic guarantees a new pairing, a new shared crime, and a new "who's editing whom" every book, with no reliance on remembering a prior cast. Readers who loved the vertigo of not knowing which narrator to trust get that exact drug reloaded with new stakes each time, under an author brand promise—"memory is the crime scene"—that's clear enough to auto-buy and elastic enough to never repeat. Rapid-release friendly because the format (two people, one dark room, one death) is a reliable, low-overhead template the author can execute fast.
concept.booktok_hook: "He came to my clinic to forget the night a man died. Halfway through his first session I realized I was there too—and one of us has been quietly rewriting the other's memory of who did it."
concept.buildability_notes: Extremely low gatekeeper burden. Setting: one contemporary boutique clinic in a small coastal Oregon town—a single recurring dim room, a couple of homes, a diner, a police-inquiry thread that stays offstage-light. Research is soft-science and public: memory reconsolidation therapy is a real, buzzy, lightly-regulated concept (propranolol/recall-under-mild-sedation studies) that a solo author can render convincingly from popular-science reading—no procedural, medical, legal, or forensic expertise required, and the 'boutique/lightly-regulated' framing sidesteps needing real clinical accuracy. Two-hander structure = tiny cast, cheap to write, easy to keep taut. ~85-90k words, 60-70 short chapters each ending on a session-reveal or a planted-detail hook. First-person present-tense in Wren's head keeps costs low and dread high. Cover: a single figure reflected imperfectly in dark glass / two faces bleeding into one, deep teal-and-black palette, coastal-noir—cheap to commission and instantly on-genre. No period, no travel, no specialized world-building—pure character-and-room pressure cooker built for rapid release.
concept.originality_claim: Vs. You/Verity/Gone Girl, the unreliability here is not a narrator lying to the reader and confessing at the end (the #1 reader complaint the brief flags)—it's TWO narrators degrading a shared memory in real time via a plausible therapy, so the distortion is diegetic, mutual, and mechanically ticking. The fresh device: a memory-reconsolidation clinic where intimacy IS the editing tool, making 'the only person who understands me is the one erasing me' literal rather than metaphorical. It sidesteps the tired affluent-suburban-marriage template entirely (working-class coastal clinic, strangers not spouses, a bond forged over a corpse rather than a mortgage), and the twist lands on a third option—neither killer nor bystander but the witness both sides needed to erase—which reframes every prior scene without cheating. That 'jointly-authored false memory as the real antagonist' is the concept's signature and its defensible, earned payoff.

title_options: Say the Part Where I Loved Her
I Was There When You Weren't
concept.logline: A death doula who moves into the homes of the dying becomes fatally obsessed with one client's wife — and now, transcribing her own confession for a true-crime podcast host she's chosen very deliberately, she narrates exactly how far she went, while never once telling you the one thing the recording is actually for.
concept.premise: Mara Vess is a live-in death doula: for a fee, she moves into the homes of the terminally ill during their final weeks, manages the medications, sits the vigils, and — she'll tell you — loves them better than their families do. The book is the full transcript of the twelve audio files Mara has recorded and sent to Dorian Ash, host of the hit true-crime podcast "Last Address," on the condition that he play them, unedited, in order. Across the tapes she confesses to the summer she spent in the coastal home of Julian Rooke, a dying architect, and to what she felt for Julian's much-younger wife, Sadie — a woman Mara insists she was only trying to protect, and whom she watched, listened to, and slowly moved to the center of her own life. Julian dies on schedule. Then Sadie's body is found in the tidal marsh below the house, and Mara is the last person who admits to being there. The tapes are Mara explaining herself: the obsession, the small trespasses that became large ones, the night on the marsh. She is precise, seductive, self-aware, and completely opaque about the one question that matters — not "did she do it," which she circles constantly, but WHY she is telling Dorian Ash specifically, in this order, right now. The reader races to the end believing they're being walked toward a murder confession. They are being walked, clue by clue, toward something else entirely.
concept.obsession_engine: Mara's obsession is professionalized proximity weaponized into intimacy. Her whole vocation is being invited into the most private weeks of a stranger's life and being needed there — she is addicted to being the one who stays when everyone else can't bear to. With Sadie, that need mutates: Mara starts by managing Julian's death and ends by curating Sadie's grief, feeding her small dependencies, learning her passwords, sleeping in the guest room listening to her cry through the wall and cataloguing it. The toxic-intimacy voltage is the reader feeling Mara's tenderness as genuine while watching it metastasize into ownership — "I loved her enough to decide what she could survive." The confession frame supercharges it: every tape is Mara performing her love for Sadie for an audience, which means we're never sure if the devotion is real or a story she's building for the microphone. The obsession drives plot because each escalation (moving a boundary in the house, intercepting a message, staying one week past the contract) is Mara feeding the addiction, and each is narrated as an act of care.
concept.protagonist_engine: Mara Vess, 39, is the obsessive confessor. We root for her despite ourselves because she is the most competent, unflinching person in any room — she does the unbearable work no one else will, and she's funny and merciless about the euphemisms families hide behind. She tells us hard truths about death that feel like intimacy, which seduces the reader into trusting her exactly as her clients do. She's psychologically opaque in the evolved way: she never lies to us outright and never saves a big confession for the last chapter; instead she withholds by reframing, narrates her trespasses as devotion, and fragments the timeline "because that's the order I can bear to say it in." Her repeatability as a series engine is baked into her job: a death doula is a professionally sanctioned reason to insert an obsessive narrator into a new household, a new dying stranger, and a new person to fixate on, every single book — the intimacy-with-the-dying premise is an infinitely refillable well.
concept.antagonist_engine: The layered antagonist is Dorian Ash, the podcast host Mara is confessing TO — and the truth of why she chose him. On the surface the opposing force is Sadie herself (the object of obsession, who turns out to have been running her own game — grooming Mara's devotion to solve her own problem: a husband who wouldn't die fast enough and a prenup that punished her if he did). But the real antagonist revealed by the frame is Dorian: Mara didn't pick a random podcaster. Dorian Ash built his career on the unsolved death of a woman years earlier — and Mara knows he was responsible for it, and has spent the whole confession feeding him a story so compelling he'll broadcast it, because the tapes are constructed so that the only way for Dorian to "solve" and air Mara's case is to expose the pattern that convicts himself. The truth is the antagonist; the confession is the weapon; Dorian is the target who doesn't know he's the target.
concept.central_threat: Two engines running at once. Front-of-mind: who killed Sadie Rooke on the marsh, and is the woman narrating the story her killer? Underneath, revealed by the frame: what is this recording actually DOING — because Mara is not confessing to be understood, she's confessing to make something happen to the man receiving it. The secret engine is that the tapes are an act, not an account: a trap disguised as a plea. The threat escalates as the reader realizes the confession they're consuming is a live weapon being deployed on Dorian in real time, and possibly on the reader-as-jury too.
concept.stakes: Personal and mortal on every layer: Sadie is dead and Mara may hang for it (or may have loved her and lost her to someone else's hand). Mara's freedom rides on how the tapes land. But the deeper stake is authorship of the truth — whoever controls the recording controls who gets blamed, and Mara has staked everything, including her own conviction, on the gamble that a broadcast confession can reach back and destroy the man who thinks he's safely on the other side of the microphone. If she's wrong about Dorian, she's handed a killer her own noose. If she's right, she trades her life to end his.
concept.escalation_pattern: Act 1 (Tapes 1-4): seduction — Mara establishes her world, her voice, her arrival at the Rooke house; small trespasses framed as care; the reader falls for both Mara and Sadie. Hook: Julian's death is not as clean as the contract promised. Act 2 (Tapes 5-8): obsession tightens — the boundaries collapse, Mara and Sadie's intimacy ignites, and Sadie's own agenda flickers at the edges; Sadie's body is found; Mara narrates the marsh night in fragments, each tape ending on a withheld beat. The reader's question flips from "will they" to "did she." Act 3 (Tapes 9-12): the frame detonates in stages — first Sadie's manipulation is exposed (Mara was used), then the reason for the podcast host is revealed (Mara chose Dorian), then the final tape recontextualizes every earlier "confession" as bait, ending on Mara's true purpose landing live. Each act reframes the tapes you already heard.
concept.twist_architecture: Three tiered, earned twists. TWIST 1 (Sadie was the operator): Mara believed she seduced/protected Sadie; in fact Sadie cultivated Mara's obsession to get Julian's death expedited and to have a devoted patsy on-site. Planted clues: Sadie's too-convenient vulnerabilities, the "accidental" oversharing of the prenup, her knowing which of Mara's buttons to press, a medication discrepancy Mara mentions and dismisses in Tape 3. TWIST 2 (Mara didn't kill Sadie — Sadie's death was Sadie's own miscalculation, or a third hand): the marsh night Mara keeps fragmenting isn't a killing she's hiding but a death she arrived too late to stop and has been narrating around out of grief and guilt. Clues: Mara's timeline gaps always cut away at the same tidal moment; her grief is real in a way a guilty narrator's performance wouldn't sustain. TWIST 3, the frame twist (WHY these tapes, to THIS host): Mara chose Dorian Ash because years earlier Dorian was the last address of another dead woman whose case made him famous, and Mara — who sat that woman's mother through her dying — learned the truth. The confession is engineered so that the forensic detail Dorian must verify to air it is the same detail that ties him to his own crime. The "why am I telling you this" that hums under all twelve tapes resolves into: because you can't resist a story this good, and this story is a hook with your name on the barb. Every seemingly self-incriminating overshare was placed to make the tapes irresistible to broadcast — the confession's opacity about its own purpose IS the twist, telegraphed by Mara's repeated, unexplained insistence that Dorian play them unedited and in order.
concept.series_engine: Standalone-complete: Sadie's death, Mara's fate, and the Dorian trap all resolve in book one. The repeatable engine is Mara Vess, death doula — the job is a license to drop an obsessive, opaque narrator into a fresh dying household and a fresh fixation every book, with a new confession-frame recipient each time (a detective, a rival doula, a dead client's child, a jury). Connected-standalone, single author-brand: each book is a new "last address," a new obsession, a new person she's telling it to for a hidden reason, a new earned twist about WHY she's telling it. No recurring couple, no cliffhanger dependency — just the brand promise: "Mara Vess confesses again, and again you won't see the barb until it's set." The world (death doulas, the ecosystem of dying, the "Last Address" podcast underworld) is a shared sandbox for future connected standalones even beyond Mara.
concept.why_buy_book_one: It delivers the You/Verity promise — an intimate, seductive, morally-grey confession you can't stop reading — but weaponizes the found-document frame harder than either: the transcript isn't just how the story is told, it's a live trap being sprung, so the form itself is the twist. Bingeable McFadden-style short tapes with brutal end-of-tape hooks, a narrator readers will fight about, a "root for someone you shouldn't" engine at full voltage, and a three-tier ending that reframes everything without cheating. High-concept, one-line pitchable, and unlike dark romance it makes no promises it won't break.
concept.why_continue: Because the final tape proves the game: Mara's confessions are irresistible AND lethal, and readers will want to watch her do it to a new target with new stakes. The pleasure is structural and repeatable — you know she's telling the story to someone for a buried reason, you know there's a barb, and you'll read the next one specifically to find it before she sets it. The death-doula premise guarantees fresh intimacy, fresh obsession, and a fresh unreliable confession every installment, all on one author brand.
concept.booktok_hook: "She's a death doula who moves in when you're dying. This is the recording she sent to a true-crime podcast confessing everything — except the real reason she's telling HIM."
concept.buildability_notes: Buildable by a solo indie fast. Contemporary coastal setting (a single house on a tidal marsh + a home studio = near-zero location research). Low gatekeeper burden: death-doula work, hospice basics, and podcast-production detail are all researchable from public sources and memoirs — no procedural cop-shop or courtroom expertise required, since the 'investigation' happens through Mara's tapes, not detectives. Structure is a gift for rapid writing: 12 audio-file 'chapters' subdividable into short scenes with built-in end-of-tape hooks; the single-POV transcript voice keeps the drafting lane narrow. ~85-90k words. The frame (transcript sent to a podcaster) naturally produces marketing copy and audiobook adaptation (it's literally an audio confession — a killer audiobook property). Series scaffolding is templated, so books 2+ reuse the doula-plus-confession-frame skeleton for faster rapid release.
concept.originality_claim: The fresh move is making the confession frame itself the twist rather than a delivery device. In You the narration is voice; in Verity the found document is evidence to be believed or doubted. Here the transcript is a WEAPON — the narrator isn't confessing to be understood or to deceive the reader about guilt, she's using the podcast broadcast as a mechanism to destroy its own recipient, so the load-bearing mystery is not 'did she do it' but 'what is this recording FOR.' Combine that with an under-used, high-intimacy profession (the live-in death doula) that gives an obsessive narrator infinite sanctioned access to strangers' most private moments, and you get an obsession thriller that reads like dark romance, resolves like a heist, and refuses the happy ending — plus a series engine that isn't a recurring couple or detective but a repeatable act of lethal storytelling.
